text.title: Release Planning Process
text.summary: Six-step structured process for bundling, scheduling, and executing product releases with cross-functional readiness verification.

section.title: Release Types

rule.statement: Classify every release as Major, Minor, Patch, or Hotfix before planning starts; the type determines ceremony level, communication scope, and rollback complexity.
rule.rationale: Type-based ceremony prevents over-engineering patch releases and under-preparing major ones. Hotfixes skip the normal process entirely and use the incident-response runbook.

section.p: Major: quarterly, large features, breaking changes, high risk. Minor: monthly, new features, medium risk. Patch: weekly or as needed, bug fixes, low risk. Hotfix: immediate, critical fixes, bypass release planning.

section.title: Step 1: Define Release Goals

rule.statement: Every release must have a primary outcome, a success metric, and optionally a theme before contents are selected.
rule.rationale: Without a goal, "what should go in this release" has no forcing function. Features get added by availability rather than coherence.

section.reference: Full release plan template including goals and metrics sections

rule.statement: Include only completed features meeting definition-of-done, related bug fixes, documentation updates, and configuration changes. Exclude half-finished features and unrelated fixes.
rule.rationale: Scope creep at release time is the leading cause of delayed deploys. Feature freeze 24-48 hours before deploy prevents last-minute additions.

rule.statement: Apply a feature freeze 24-48 hours before the planned deploy; no new items after freeze without explicit PM exception logged in the release exceptions log.
rule.rationale: Post-freeze additions bypass the readiness gate and introduce untested surface area into the release.

section.title: Step 3: Assess Release Readiness

rule.statement: Gate release on a per-function readiness matrix (eng, docs, support, marketing, legal, GTM). Green requires an evidence URL. Yellow and red require a named owner and blocker.
rule.rationale: A "release plan complete" status that masks yellow/red cells ships unready features. Evidence URLs make green cells falsifiable, not aspirational.

section.reference: Script that validates the readiness matrix: green rows must have evidence URLs, yellow/red must have named owners.

section.title: Step 4: Plan Communication

rule.statement: Tag every manifest item as customer_visible (Y/N) and breaking_change (Y/N). Items with customer_visible=N skip the comms loop entirely.
rule.rationale: Applying the full comms plan to infra-only releases inflates PM workload without customer benefit. The boolean forces deliberate opt-in per item.

section.reference: Customer-facing release notes template with required sections and anti-fluff rules.
LLM prompt for drafting release notes with anti-fluff discipline.

rule.statement: Deploy Monday through Wednesday; never deploy on Fridays or before holidays without a documented exception in the release exceptions log.
rule.rationale: Friday deploys reduce the on-call response window and increase incident blast radius. Documented exceptions create an audit trail for policy decay detection.

rule.statement: Monitor metrics for 24 hours post-deploy. Hold a post-release retrospective; outputs update the next release's risk register with named owners.
rule.rationale: Retrospective outputs tied to the next release's risk register turn one-off incidents into systemic improvements rather than forgotten post-mortems.

antipattern.description: Ship "one more thing" after feature freeze because it is "already built".
antipattern.reason: Late additions bypass the readiness matrix and are the most common source of post-release regressions.
